content.text: Flask is a lightweight Python web framework that gives you the essentials — routing, request parsing, response building, and a template engine — without imposing a project structure or forcing you to use specific libraries. Its simplicity has made it one of the most popular Python web frameworks for over a decade, and it remains an excellent choice for simple agent API endpoints, internal tools, and projects that do not require the async concurrency of FastAPI.

Flask is a micro-framework built on two libraries: Werkzeug (the WSGI utility toolkit providing URL routing, request/response objects, and a development server) and Jinja2 (the template engine for rendering HTML). Flask itself provides the glue — an application object, a routing decorator system, request context management, and a plugin mechanism (extensions). Everything else (database access, authentication, validation, serialisation) is provided by extensions or chosen by the developer.

Flask is a WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) framework. Every request is handled synchronously: a worker process calls your route function, which runs to completion (blocking on any I/O along the way), and returns a response. This model is simple to reason about but limits concurrency under heavy load or long-running LLM calls. Each Gunicorn worker can handle only one request at a time, so 4 workers handle at most 4 simultaneous requests.

Flask was created by Armin Ronacher and first released in 2010 as an April Fools' joke that people took seriously. It grew into one of the most downloaded Python packages, used in production by Pinterest, LinkedIn, Lyft, and many others. For agentic AI development, Flask works well for prototyping, internal tools, simple webhook receivers, low-traffic agent trigger endpoints, and services where the team already has strong Flask expertise.

attrs.alt: Flask application architecture: WSGI stack, routing, blueprints, request context, and extensions

content.text: Flask's value proposition is learnable simplicity. A new developer can read the Flask documentation in an afternoon and understand the complete framework — there are no hidden magic patterns or complex abstractions to learn. A minimal Flask application is five lines of code. This low barrier to entry makes Flask an excellent starting point for agentic AI projects, especially when the team is learning both Python web development and LLM integration simultaneously.

Beyond learning, Flask's explicit-over-implicit philosophy produces code that is easy to read months later. In a FastAPI application, a route handler may depend on multiple injected resources through the
system with lifecycle management you need to trace through the code. In Flask, the same dependencies are usually accessed directly via the request context (
) or imported from modules. This is less structured but immediately readable without knowing the framework's DI conventions.

Flask's extension ecosystem solves the integration problems that WSGI synchronous applications face with modern AI workloads. Flask-SocketIO adds WebSocket support. Celery handles long-running agent tasks in a queue. Flask-Caching adds response caching. Flask-Limiter provides rate limiting. These extensions are mature, well-documented, and cover the patterns needed to build production-grade agentic services on WSGI.
the core Flask API is small and learnable in hours. Route decorators, request context, and jsonify are self-explanatory.
explicitly, you write your own validation. Nothing is hidden.
Flask does not dictate your project layout, ORM, or serialisation library. You choose what you need.
Flask-SQLAlchemy, Flask-JWT-Extended, Flask-CORS, Flask-Limiter, Flask-Migrate, Flask-Caching — a mature ecosystem for every common need.
14 years of tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, and production examples. Almost any problem has a documented solution.
Flask's test client makes it easy to write integration tests without spinning up a server. The application factory pattern enables per-test app configuration.
for endpoints that do synchronous operations (database reads, simple transformations, webhook receivers), WSGI synchronous code is easier to debug and profile.
route support via Asgiref, but async routes in Flask still run on a threadpool under WSGI — there is no event loop. True async concurrency requires migrating to ASGI (FastAPI/Starlette).
a Flask worker running a 30-second LLM call blocks that worker for 30 seconds. Scale by adding workers (costly in memory) rather than via async concurrency.
unlike FastAPI, Flask does not validate request bodies automatically. You must call
and validate the result manually or add a validation library (Marshmallow, Pydantic).
Flask does not generate Swagger UI or OpenAPI specs by default. Adding flask-openapi3 or flasgger requires configuration that FastAPI provides automatically.
Flask supports chunked transfer encoding via generators and
, but SSE streaming requires keeping worker threads occupied for the stream duration, limiting concurrency.
) is thread-local global state, which can cause subtle bugs in testing and when using concurrency primitives.
A Flask application starts by creating an instance of the
argument helps Flask find template and static files relative to the module. Route decorators (
) register URL patterns and their associated view functions in a
When a request arrives at a WSGI server (Gunicorn), the server calls
with the HTTP request environment. Flask creates a request context that pushes
proxy objects onto a thread-local stack. It then matches the request path and method against the URL map, calls the matched view function, and wraps the return value in a
Flask's request context is essential to understand. The
proxy object always refers to the current request in the current thread. Accessing
outside a request context (in a background thread or Celery task, for example) raises a
RuntimeError: working outside of request context
to push an application context manually when you need Flask's configuration outside a request.

Flask applications are best organised using the application factory pattern and blueprints. The application factory is a function that creates and configures the Flask app — this enables different configurations for testing, development, and production, and prevents circular import issues.

Blueprints are Flask's module system for organising routes. A blueprint is a collection of routes with a shared URL prefix, error handlers, and before/after request hooks. Blueprints are registered on the app object at startup. Well-designed Flask applications separate concerns into blueprints: one for agent endpoints, one for tool management, one for authentication, one for admin.
You are prototyping an agent endpoint quickly and want to move fast without configuration overhead.
You are building an internal tool or admin dashboard where concurrency is low (fewer than 10 simultaneous users) and the team knows Flask well.
You are adding LLM capabilities to an existing Flask application and the migration cost to FastAPI outweighs the async concurrency benefit.
You are building a simple webhook receiver that processes events synchronously and does not need to stream responses.
You are teaching web API development alongside LLM integration — Flask's explicitness makes it easier to understand what is happening at each step.

Flask is less appropriate when: you need to stream LLM tokens to many concurrent users (use FastAPI + ASGI instead); your agent handles more than 10 simultaneous long-running sessions (ASGI scales better); or you need automatic request validation and OpenAPI docs with no extra code (FastAPI provides these).

# Install Flask and a production server
pip install flask gunicorn

# For API development with common extras:
pip install flask flask-cors flask-limiter python-dotenv

# Run development server (NOT for production):
flask --app main run --debug

# Run with Gunicorn (production):
gunicorn main:app --workers 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
Basic routes, request parsing, and error handling
Blueprints and the application factory pattern
Request context: g, request, session, and hooks
Streaming LLM responses via SSE in Flask

from flask import Flask, Response, request, stream_with_context
import anthropic, json

app = Flask(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()   # sync client — Flask is WSGI

@app.route("/api/v1/stream", methods=["POST"])
def stream_completion():
    data = request.get_json(force=True)
    prompt = data.get("prompt", "")
    model  = data.get("model", "claude-opus-4-5")

    def generate():
        """Generator that yields SSE-formatted events."""
        yield "data: [START]\n\n"

        # Anthropic SDK streaming — sync, but yields incrementally
        with client.messages.stream(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=2048,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        ) as stream:
            for text in stream.text_stream:
                payload = json.dumps({"token": text})
                yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

            # Send final message with usage stats
            final = stream.get_final_message()
            usage = {
                "input_tokens":  final.usage.input_tokens,
                "output_tokens": final.usage.output_tokens,
            }
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'done': True, 'usage': usage})}\n\n"

    # stream_with_context keeps Flask request context alive during iteration
    return Response(
        stream_with_context(generate()),
        mimetype="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable nginx output buffering
        },
    )
Reusable decorators for validation and rate limiting

import os
from flask import Flask
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Config:
    ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: str = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "")
    DATABASE_URL: str      = os.environ.get("DATABASE_URL", "sqlite:///dev.db")
    SECRET_KEY: str        = os.environ.get("SECRET_KEY", "dev-only-change-me")
    DEBUG: bool            = os.environ.get("FLASK_DEBUG", "false").lower() == "true"
    RATELIMIT_STORAGE_URI: str = os.environ.get("REDIS_URL", "memory://")

def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    config = Config()

    # Set Flask config from dataclass
    app.config["SECRET_KEY"] = config.SECRET_KEY
    app.config["DEBUG"] = config.DEBUG

    # Store typed config for use in routes
    app.config["APP_CONFIG"] = config

    # Initialise extensions with the app
    from flask_cors import CORS
    CORS(app, resources={r"/api/*": {"origins": ["https://yourdomain.com"]}})

    from flask_limiter import Limiter
    from flask_limiter.util import get_remote_address
    limiter = Limiter(
        app=app,
        key_func=get_remote_address,
        storage_uri=config.RATELIMIT_STORAGE_URI,
        default_limits=["200 per day", "60 per hour"],
    )

    # Register blueprints
    from .routes.agents import agents_bp
    from .routes.runs   import runs_bp
    app.register_blueprint(agents_bp)
    app.register_blueprint(runs_bp)

    return app
Many early LLM-powered startups built their first product on Flask. A typical pattern: a

endpoint receives a message, calls the Anthropic Messages API synchronously, and returns the response. This worked fine at 1-5 concurrent users during a beta. As the product grew to 50+ concurrent users, worker slots started saturating because each 10-second LLM call occupied a worker throughout.

The first scaling fix is usually to increase Gunicorn workers. Going from 4 to 16 workers handles 16 simultaneous LLM calls but uses 4x more memory. The second fix is to move LLM calls to Celery background tasks: the Flask endpoint creates a job and returns a job ID immediately, and a Celery worker pool processes the LLM calls asynchronously. This is a viable production architecture for Flask — it decouples web request handling from LLM latency and scales each tier independently.

The cleanest long-term path for Flask applications that need high concurrency is migration to FastAPI. Because both frameworks use function-based routing with similar decorator syntax, the migration is often mechanical: change
with a Pydantic model parameter, replace
with a return dict, and add
to route functions that await I/O.
defining routes directly on a module-level
makes it impossible to create test instances with different config. Always use a factory function.
) is single-threaded, has no process management, and is not safe for production. Always use Gunicorn or uWSGI.

returning a Python dict from a route works in Flask 1.0+, but does not set
in older versions and is less explicit. Use
Not handling errors for all 4xx and 5xx codes:
without registered error handlers, Flask returns bare HTML error pages. Always register handlers for at least 400, 404, 422, and 500 that return JSON.
and type checks in every handler creates inconsistent error messages. Centralise validation with Marshmallow schemas or Pydantic models applied in a decorator.
Storing state in g across requests:
is request-scoped and reset after every response. Never use it to store data you need in the next request. Use the session cookie or a database for persistent state.
Flask's session cookies are HMAC-signed with
. A weak or default key allows session forgery. Load
from an environment variable or secret manager in production.
Use the application factory pattern: define
and call it in your WSGI entry point. This enables per-environment configuration and isolated test setups.
Use blueprints for all route groups. Never define more than a few routes directly on the main app object.
. A request with the wrong content type returns
Register error handlers for 400, 401, 403, 404, 422, and 500 that return structured JSON — not Flask's default HTML error pages.
Keep route handlers thin. Call service functions that contain business logic. Route handlers should only parse inputs, call services, and format responses.
Use environment variables for all configuration. Load them with
in development and from secrets management in production.
as a starting formula for CPU-bound workloads. Reduce workers for I/O-heavy (LLM-calling) workloads to reduce memory.
Write tests using Flask's test client (
) for integration tests. Use pytest fixtures to create a fresh app instance per test with test configuration.
Flask's performance is bounded by the number of Gunicorn workers (or threads with the threaded worker class). For CPU-bound workloads, the rule of thumb is

workers. For I/O-bound workloads like LLM calls, more workers help because each worker spends most of its time waiting on the network. A realistic production configuration for a Flask agent API is 4-8 workers, each handling up to one concurrent LLM call, giving 4-8 simultaneous agent sessions.

) provides cooperative multitasking within a single worker process. Gevent monkey-patches Python's standard socket operations to be non-blocking, allowing a single worker to handle many concurrent I/O-bound requests. This is an intermediate solution between WSGI concurrency and full ASGI migration — it works well for LLM calls made through

For production Flask agent services, move long-running agent executions to Celery workers. The Flask endpoint receives the request, creates a Celery task, and immediately returns a job ID. The Celery worker pool independently runs the LLM calls. The client polls a status endpoint. This architecture scales the web tier and the agent execution tier independently and adds durability (Celery tasks survive web server restarts).

Flask response caching with Flask-Caching reduces load for expensive read operations. Cache tool definitions, model metadata, and user profiles in Redis with a short TTL. A well-cached Flask API can handle much higher read load than a naively implemented one.

serialisation and deserialisation library that works with Flask to validate request bodies and serialise response objects. Provides field-level error messages similar to Pydantic.
distributed task queue for running long-running agent jobs outside the Flask request cycle. Integrates with Flask via the application factory.
write API integration tests by calling
. Load this from an environment variable or secrets manager. Rotating the key invalidates all existing sessions.
Flask provides no built-in request body validation. Always validate and sanitise all inputs manually or via Marshmallow/Pydantic before passing them to business logic or database queries.
use SQLAlchemy's ORM or parameterised queries. Never build SQL strings by interpolating request data.
for browser-facing endpoints using session cookies, use Flask-WTF's CSRF protection. API endpoints using token authentication are not vulnerable to CSRF.
Flask's default error handlers return HTML pages that reveal framework versions and sometimes stack traces. Register JSON error handlers for all error codes and disable DEBUG mode in production.
Flask and Werkzeug do not limit request body size by default. Configure
Flask does not handle TLS. Terminate TLS at nginx or a load balancer. Set
) longer than your LLM call duration but short enough to prevent resource exhaustion from hung connections.

Flask is a decorator-driven routing engine that wraps Werkzeug's request/response toolkit. Imagine your application as a receptionist at a front desk. The URL map is the receptionist's directory — when a request arrives, the receptionist looks up the URL and method, finds the right function to call, gives that function access to the request details via the request context (
), and returns the response to the caller.

The application factory and blueprints are the organisational equivalent of departments within a building. Each blueprint is a department with its own phone extension prefix (URL prefix), its own set of staff (route functions), and its own internal rules (before/after_request hooks scoped to the blueprint). The factory creates the building with all departments installed and configured, making it easy to create test buildings with different layouts.
Extensions plug into Flask's signal system: they register
handlers, application context teardown functions, and CLI commands when you call
pattern exists — it lets extensions register themselves with whichever app factory is active, enabling the same extension instance to serve multiple application instances (useful in testing).
Flask is a lightweight WSGI micro-framework — minimal, explicit, and learnable in a day.
) and blueprints for any project beyond a single file.
) is thread-local and only valid inside a request. Pass data explicitly to background jobs.
Flask does not validate request bodies automatically. Use Marshmallow or Pydantic manually, or add a validation decorator.
For high concurrency with long LLM calls, move agent execution to Celery workers and use the polling pattern.
with the gevent worker class to avoid blocking all workers during stream delivery.
Deploy with Gunicorn in production. Never use
When concurrency requirements outgrow WSGI's model, migrate to FastAPI — the route decorator syntax is similar enough that the migration is usually mechanical.
